story.description: A clip of farm animals: Mallard Duck Swims By Camera (Wide Screen)
story.hook: Ducks keep water sliding off their feathers
story.lead: Duck swims by camera. Notice how its eye stays dry above the waterline. That's because it’s got a clear nictitating membrane—a built-in third eyelid sliding across like a tiny windshield. Keeps the view steady while it paddles because birds can’t blink like we do. Ever wonder why ducks bob their heads when they walk. Same reason: they hold their eye still to pick up detail. So next time you see a
story.script: Duck swims by camera. Notice how its eye stays dry above the waterline. That's because it’s got a clear nictitating membrane—a built-in third eyelid sliding across like a tiny windshield. Keeps the view steady while it paddles because birds can’t blink like we do. Ever wonder why ducks bob their heads when they walk. Same reason: they hold their eye still to pick up detail. So next time you see a mallard, watch its eye not its tail.

story.description: A clip of plants, leaves, flowers, or plant movement: Photomicrography reveals the unusual structure and behavior of the Venus's flytrap, the trumpet plant, the cobra plant, the common pitcher plant, the sundew plant and the utricularia.
story.hook: Plants show the first clue before the payoff.
story.lead: This plant snaps faster than your reflexes. Watch the inside hairs bend, that's the trigger. Because the trap must close fast to trap insects before they escape. That's why it’s spring-loaded—like a mousetrap with muscles. Ever wondered why it doesn’t snap shut on raindrops. Coz the hairs only fire if two are touched within 20 seconds. What would you name that trigger.
story.script: This plant snaps faster than your reflexes. Watch the inside hairs bend, that's the trigger. Because the trap must close fast to trap insects before they escape. That's why it’s spring-loaded—like a mousetrap with muscles. Ever wondered why it doesn’t snap shut on raindrops. Coz the hairs only fire if two are touched within 20 seconds. What would you name that trigger.

story.description: A clip of space, planets, solar activity, rockets, satellites, or astronomy: A Coke Bottle and Drinking Flask are Seen in The Fake Moon Bay -Astronauts Had Hidden Holes in Their Helmets For Breathing. Apollo 11 photo TV still, located at space agency site: http://spaceflight.space agency.gov/gallery/images/apollo/apollo11/hires/s69_40308.jpg space agency photo as you see it, can be downloaded from space agency site: http://www.apolloarchive.com/apollo_gallery.html The Photo Number is: Apollo 17 AS17-134-20453 Classical music in this video is copyright free public domain, available to download and use f
story.hook: Earth shows the first clue before the payoff.
story.lead: That bright patch isn't a flag—it's glare from the lunar surface. Because the moon's dust is like shattered glass, it bounces sunlight straight back at cameras. That's why Apollo 11 photos show this ghostly white bay. Watch it twice and see the horizon curve—moon's gravity keeps it tight. Ever notice how Earth's glare looks different?
story.script: That bright patch isn't a flag—it's glare from the lunar surface. Because the moon's dust is like shattered glass, it bounces sunlight straight back at cameras. That's why Apollo 11 photos show this ghostly white bay. Watch it twice and see the horizon curve—moon's gravity keeps it tight. Ever notice how Earth's glare looks different?
